children.text: *	The present document (A/81/6 (Sect. 29F)/Part A), consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II; see General Assembly resolution 77/267, para. 10), will be replaced by a document containing parts A and B (A/81/6 (Sect. 29F)) once part B is issued.

***	In keeping with paragraph 10 of General Assembly resolution 77/267, the part consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II) is submitted through the Committee for Programme and Coordination for the consideration of the Assembly.

text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29F.1	The United Nations Office at Vienna was established in 1979 pursuant to General Assembly resolution 31/194 with the objective of providing administrative support to the United Nations Secretariat units located in Vienna, including the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC), the Office for Outer Space Affairs, the International Trade Law Division of the Office of Legal Affairs, the secretariat of the United Nations Scientific Committee on the Effects of Atomic Radiation, the United Nations Information Service, the Office of Internal Oversight Services, the Office for Disarmament Affairs, the regional office of the Office of the United Nations Ombudsman and Mediation Services and the United Nations Register of Damage Caused by the Construction of the Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory. Under the terms of the tripartite memorandum of understanding of 1977 and subsequent amendments to it, the Office also provides support and administrative services, garage operations, language training and communication services on a common services basis to other international organizations based in the Vienna International Centre, namely, the International Atomic Energy Agency, the United Nations Industrial Development Organization and the Preparatory Commission for the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization. The Office provides safety and security and conference management services to the Vienna-based organizations, as reflected in section 34 and section 2, respectively, of the proposed programme budget. Building management services at the Vienna International Centre are provided by the United Nations Industrial Development Organization and medical services are provided by the Agency.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29F.2	The Office will continue to provide administrative services, including the provision of an enabling policy framework and enhanced decision-making through continuous monitoring, data analysis and reporting. The strategy for 2027 will continue to include: (a) coordinating with Headquarters and providing timely advice to the entities serviced on policy and administrative matters; (b) strengthening internal financial controls and streamlining financial procedures and guidelines to ensure effective financial management; (c) targeting the enhancement of leadership, learning and career support activities guided by a performance management system supporting increased accountability, staff engagement, workplace flexibility and empowerment while ensuring a diverse and inclusive workplace; (d) maintaining efficient and cost-effective travel and transportation services through best practices; (e) promoting environmental sustainability in coordination with all departments and offices; (f) strengthening the capacity of procurement staff through online training and guidance notes to procurement practitioners; (g) monitoring significant pronouncements of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards Board and improving financial data integrity; (h) integrating new technologies into the organizational business toolkit; and (i) increasing compliance with technology standards, guidelines and methodologies, information and communications technology (ICT) policies and enterprise architecture.

29F.3	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that the extrabudgetary funding available to client offices and the support requirements for services provided by the Office remain stable.

29F.4	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, including through the implementation of the Strategy for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (2022–2026) of the Office and UNODC. It will also support clients in attaining gender parity targets and will track and report on progress in promoting gender equality and the empowerment of women, in line with the Secretary-General’s system-wide strategy on gender parity.

29F.5	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office, in coordination with other Vienna-based organizations, will continue to work towards creating a more inclusive and supportive culture for staff members who have disabilities. The Office will engage with and involve persons with disabilities and their representative organizations, offer training on disability inclusion and raise awareness on disability inclusion.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

29F.6	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. For example, in the light of the consolidation of payroll processing into a single global team, the Office will cease payroll operations, as reflected in the updated table of deliverables of subprogramme 1. In addition, in response to the changing staff footprint in Vienna following the abolishment of posts and the relocation of staff from higher-cost duty stations, the Office will review office space utilization, as reflected in result 3 under subprogramme 3. The Office also reduced the number of language courses in each of the six official languages provided under subprogramme 2.

29F.7	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals relating to the establishment of a common administrative platform in Vienna, as part of the next phase of the consolidation and streamlining of support services and structures across the Secretariat.
Legislative mandates
29F.8	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.
General Assembly resolutions

children.text: Evaluation activities
29F.10	An evaluation of the accessibility of the Vienna International Centre, conducted by the Office and completed in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

29F.11	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Office will implement structural accessibility recommendations to improve access to persons with disabilities, including in the context of the workspace capacity improvements, as outlined under result 3 of subprogramme 3.
29F.12	An evaluation of the due diligence process undertaken in assessing potential partners, to be conducted by the Office, is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Programme planning, finance and budget
Objective
29F.13	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure sound, effective and efficient financial management by client offices.
Strategy
29F.14	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Strengthen the monitoring of budget performance, financial management and control, and improve reporting;
(b)	Provide advice, assistance, guidance and training to programme managers and administrative units of client offices on financial management and budgetary matters;
(c)	Streamline financial processes and guidelines and regularly review workflow procedures to identify ways to achieve a more efficient and faster client response;
(d)	Strengthen internal financial controls, monitor financial performance and risks and ensure the integrity of organizational financial data;

(e)	Monitor the implementation of recommendations from oversight bodies and ensure strict compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and the financial policies of the United Nations.
29F.15	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved accessibility and understanding of financial information by client offices;
(b)	Increased accountability by client offices;
(c)	Improved integrity of financial data;
(d)	Enhanced overall management of programme budget and extrabudgetary financial resources.
Programme performance in 2025
Maintaining programme delivery amid unexpected funding disruption

29F.16	In 2025, one of the Office’s client entities, UNODC, faced a decrease in financial resources both from the regular budget due to the liquidity situation and from extrabudgetary resources due to reductions in voluntary contributions from several donors. This decrease in resources posed a significant challenge to the continuity of UNODC operations and its ability to achieve its mandates, including its ability to provide technical cooperation to Member States.

29F.17	The subprogramme assisted UNODC by providing more frequent financial updates to senior management for decision-making and keeping Member States and donors engaged through the standing open-ended intergovernmental working group on improving the governance and financial situation of UNODC. The subprogramme held regular meetings with UNODC management and programme managers on contingency scenarios and the reallocation of available resources to areas of greater need. Consequently, planned activities continued with minimal interruption, notwithstanding significant funding uncertainty.
29F.18	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29F.2).
Table 29F.2
Performance measure

table_content.text: Standard information available to stakeholders for decision-making
Standard information available to stakeholders for decision-making
Decision-making by managers enabled through the increased frequency of tailored information on the impact of the financial situation on projects and advice on mitigation measures

children.text: Result 3: improved financial transparency and management oversight – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29F.23	Effective financial management requires timely, reliable and accessible information to support decision-making and accountability. In the light of the financial challenges experienced in recent years, the subprogramme has faced increasing demands for transparency and oversight through frequent ad hoc requests for key consolidated financial information and reports.
Lessons learned and planned change

29F.24	The lesson for the subprogramme was that fragmented and manual reporting limited the timeliness, consistency and usability of financial information for decision-makers. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme is developing standardized financial management dashboards using analytical application tools, including dedicated views for executive management and for operational managers and staff. These dashboards will be designed to consolidate key financial information and support the monitoring of financial performance across clients’ offices. The subprogramme will publish and progressively refine the dashboards on the basis of user feedback and will increase the automation of data preparation through Umoja Analytics, thereby enhancing efficiency, reliability and user relevance.
29F.25	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29F.5).

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Human resources management
Objective

29F.27	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the attraction, development and retention of a competent, diverse, adaptable and healthy workforce with the highest standards of efficiency, competence and integrity, giving due regard to geographical representation, inclusivity and gender balance.
Strategy
29F.28	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Foster and reinforce an enabling working environment for its clients with regard to service delivery, talent management and organizational culture through human resources services to clients in Vienna and in UNODC field offices;
(b)	Implement talent outreach and attraction initiatives to enhance gender parity, equitable geographical distribution and disability inclusion;
(c)	Support personnel through talent development initiatives;

(d)	Reinforce a culture of zero tolerance against prohibited conduct, including through the implementation of multiple initiatives of the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination.
29F.29	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in improved staff engagement, motivation, productivity and well-being, thereby contributing to the delivery of the Organization’s mandates.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved contractual modality for field-based project personnel

29F.30	Ensuring the full alignment of benefits and entitlements for field-based project personnel across the Office’s clients located in 116 duty stations is essential to promoting fairness, transparency and staff motivation and supporting the effective implementation of projects funded through Member States’ voluntary contributions. In 2025, the Office worked closely with UNODC to ensure the transition of the service provider of field service contracts from the United Nations Development Programme to the United Nations Office for Project Service (UNOPS) and, in collaboration with UNOPS, developed a dedicated formal agreement, a contractual framework and related policies and procedures tailored to operational needs. Consequently, in December 2025, 1,247 service contract holders previously administered by the United Nations Development Programme underwent a successful transition to the UNOPS local individual contractor agreement modality, ensuring continuity of operations and mandate delivery while providing improved and more harmonized employment conditions for project personnel.
29F.31	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29F.7).
Table 29F.7
Performance measure

table_content.text: A total of 1,247 service contractors made the successful transition to the UNOPS local individual contractor agreement modality tailored to United Nations Office at Vienna/UNODC needs

Figure 29F.I
Performance measure: number of views recorded on the human resources newsletters (annual)
(Total annual number)
Result 3: increased awareness of the Code of Conduct to Prevent Harassment, Including Sexual Harassment, at United Nations System Events – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29F.36	The subprogramme supports efforts to ensure that all staff and personnel are treated with dignity and respect and are aware of their roles and responsibilities in maintaining a workplace free of any form of discrimination, harassment, including sexual harassment, and abuse of authority. In addition, the subprogramme seeks to ensure that events organized by client departments provide an inclusive, respectful and safe environment for all participants. United Nations staff and personnel are directed by the Secretary General’s bulletin on addressing discrimination, harassment, including sexual harassment and abuse of authority (ST/SGB/2019/8), while the Code of Conduct to Prevent Harassment, Including Sexual Harassment, at United Nations System Events applies to external visitors at events organized by the United Nations. Procedures are in place to report any prohibited conduct. Organizers of events are expected to take appropriate action in accordance with applicable policies.
Lessons learned and planned change

29F.37	The lesson for the subprogramme was that a more systematic approach was required to ensure that all participants in events organized by its client departments were aware of the Code of Conduct to Prevent Harassment, Including Sexual Harassment, at United Nations System Events, including on how to report any perceived prohibited conduct. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will work closely with event organizers to expand the awareness of the Code of Conduct through the revamped and increased distribution of information materials and announcements at the onset of the events. The subprogramme will also strengthen review mechanisms to enable an immediate review of any reports of prohibited conduct.
29F.38	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29F.II).
Figure 29F.II

Performance measure: percentage of surveyed respondents who are aware of the Code of Conduct to Prevent Harassment, Including Sexual Harassment, at United Nations System Events and know how to report prohibited conduct (annual)

table_content.text: Outreach programmes, special events and information materials: 20 targeted outreach programmes, including special events aligned with the system-wide strategy, information and broadcast materials, job fairs and career presentations for approximately 1,500 potential candidates.

Administration: human resources services to 3,000 personnel; classification and reclassification of more than 400 posts, outreach and recruitment for 200 staff posts, 2,000 non-staff engagement processes, human resources policies and legal advice; staff counselling; talent management; issuance and renewal of appointments and contracts; training on language in the 6 official languages and organizational and managerial competencies; facilitation of access to the global orientation programme (Go2UN); medical services in collaboration with other Vienna-based entities; and awareness-raising programmes on prohibited conduct and the Enabling Environment Guidelines for the United Nations System for all staff.

Internal justice and oversight: 1,000 requests for legal and policy advice to colleagues in the discharge of their official functions, and advice to senior officials and staff on the application of the Staff Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and other administrative issuances, including legal positions for administrative reviews and cases brought by staff to the Management Advice and Evaluation Section and the Office of the United Nations Ombudsman and Mediation Services; assistance in receiving, assessing and processing complaints and reports of alleged misconduct; discharging the role of the conduct and discipline focal point; and awareness-raising on matters related to the administration of justice at the United Nations.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Support services
Objective

29F.40	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the efficient, environmentally sound and effective functioning of client offices with regard to conference and facilities management, property management, travel and transportation, mail and pouch services, procurement, engagement of implementing partners, commercial activities, records management and library services.
Strategy
29F.41	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Manage an effective supply chain by building on the related Umoja functionality;
(b)	Implement an environmental management system in line with the United Nations Secretariat environmental policy;
(c)	Continue to improve the business continuity approach through further integration with other parts of the organizational resilience management system;
(d)	Provide property management services, diplomatic pouch and mail services and manage records;
(e)	Provide various travel-related services, including advice, training and policy guidance;
(f)	Provide process and policy guidance on the engagement of implementing partners;
(g)	Provide a more flexible and inclusive workspace;
(h)	Engage in outreach on library services.
29F.42	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Effective and sustainable use of United Nations property, including office space;
(b)	Effective management of clients’ travel resources;
(c)	An effective environmental management system;
(d)	Increased use of information resources contained in the library for informed decision-making by clients and Member States;
(e)	Improved planning and sourcing of all goods and services;
(f)	A robust business continuity plan;
(g)	Timely and accurate management of documents and mail.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened property management

29F.43	The effective management of property is critical to safeguarding United Nations assets, strengthening accountability and transparency and ensuring value for money by optimizing asset use and reducing loss, misuse and replacement costs. To strengthen local accountability and improve the speed of recording, the Office decentralized property management to its clients’ field offices. Through regional training in Bangkok, Bogotá, Nairobi and Tashkent, more than 500 staff gained hands-on Umoja skills to manage assets end to end. Consequently, field assets are managed locally and more independently. As of 2025, 89 countries operated under this decentralized model.
29F.44	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29F.10).
Table 29F.10
Performance measure

table_content.text: Centralized management of United Nations property with delays and weak local accountability
Regional offices participate in hands-on training on property management
Strengthened local accountability for property management in 89 countries, with timely recording and tracking of non-capitalized property

children.text: Result 3: improved utilization of office space – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29F.49	The Vienna International Centre was inaugurated in 1979 to host United Nations organizations based in Vienna, including the Office and its client organizations. Since then, while personnel numbers have increased steadily, the allocated spatial footprint within the Centre has not expanded. The limitations of the existing office layout are further compounded by changes arising from the UN80 Initiative, with personnel relocating to Vienna from higher-cost duty stations, as well as the need to accommodate a newly established entity.
Lessons learned and planned change

29F.50	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to assess space utilization in accordance with the office space standards of the Office/UNODC to increase workspace capacity. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme conducted a baseline assessment of office space utilization in 2025 and will launch a project to increase workspace capacity. This will be achieved by implementing a more flexible use of space, while taking into account accessibility requirements for persons with disabilities, as well as safety and fire regulations, with a view to ensuring optimal space efficiency and an inclusive working environment.
29F.51	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29F.IV).
Figure 29F.IV
Performance measure: number of workstations available for staff and personnel (cumulative)
Deliverables
29F.52	Table 29F.12 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29F.12
Subprogramme 3: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Library services: knowledge services for 3,270 library clients; digitized official documents available online; and print and electronic information resources with on-site and online access.

Administration: travel arrangements (46,800), visas (210) and laissez-passer (200) for staff and meeting participants for Vienna-based United Nations Secretariat entities and more than 140 UNODC field office locations; active agreements with implementing partners (308); and management and maintenance of the United Nations Office at Vienna (total footprint of approximately 16,500 m2).

Logistics: full procurement services for 2,600 items; physical verification of 8,975 assets and equipment; monitoring of United Nations property records at the Vienna International Centre and 140 UNODC field office locations; shipment of official property, bulk consignments for conferences and removal of personal effects and related insurance requirement for staff; and mail, pouch and distribution services of approximately 49,700 items (or 70 tons).

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Information and communications technology operations
Objective

29F.53	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the efficient and effective implementation of client entities’ substantive programmes through technologies, including a coherent enterprise ICT system and enterprise architecture, access to ICT-related technological innovations and a secure, coherent and resilient technology application hosting and infrastructure landscape.
Strategy
29F.54	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Place client offices’ programme objectives and business needs at the centre of decisions involving ICT service offerings and the implementation of new technologies;
(b)	Support enterprise application deployments and integrate local service delivery mechanisms to avoid a replication of globally available services;

(c)	Strengthen security in the use of ICT and data protection through the implementation of vulnerability management, continuous monitoring, the protection of classified information and related policies and procedures;

(d)	Promote cloud computing through the use of Secretariat-wide systems contracts and increase compliance with technology standards, guidelines and methodologies by cooperating with the Office of Information and Communications Technology.
29F.55	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Clients having improved user experience in ICT service delivery;
(b)	Conformance with global United Nations Secretariat standards and technical procedures;
(c)	Clients who are empowered during the decision-making process and whose administrative and management tasks are facilitated through access to appropriate information technology tools;

(d)	Reduced fragmentation of digital tools and services, with staff incorporating tools and services seamlessly into their daily business workflows with increased effectiveness, efficiency and satisfaction;
(e)	Greater scalability, with systems that are more adaptable to changing needs.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened reliability of information and communications technology operations through the consolidation of virtual infrastructure and a reduced reliance on subscription-based licences

29F.56	The Office relies on data centre operations to support Member States, intergovernmental bodies and the safe and secure operation of the Vienna International Centre. Ensuring the reliability, resilience and sustainability of these operations is therefore essential to guaranteeing uninterrupted services for the Office’s clients and minimizing the risk of system instability resulting from rapidly evolving technologies and shifting licensing models. Between 2023 and 2025, the subprogramme modernized data centre operations by consolidating fragmented and ageing infrastructure into a more resilient and centrally managed environment. This consolidation reduced the number of systems to be maintained and lowered exposure to hardware failures, thereby improving the Organization’s ability to cope with demand fluctuations, while limiting the risk of sudden increases in maintenance and energy. Consequently, the Office/UNODC strengthened the reliability of ICT operations and established a more robust foundation for future technology decisions.
29F.57	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29F.13).
Table 29F.13
Performance measure

table_content.text: Strengthened reliability of ICT operations, with a 32.5 per cent reduction in hardware footprint compared with 2023

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: strengthened information security in the field – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29F.58	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 61 per cent of field personnel being able to identify common scenario threats and 27 security incidents being reported, which exceeded the planned targets of 55 per cent and 20, respectively.
29F.59	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29F.V).
Figure 29F.V
Performance measure: percentage of field personnel able to identify common scenario threats and number of reports by personnel of potential serious security incidents
Result 2: digital transformation by client entities for improved mandate delivery – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29F.60	The subprogramme’s work contributed to progress towards the planned target for 2026, with the initial development of an innovation and digital transformation road map to be implemented by client entities.
29F.61	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29F.VI).
Figure 29F.VI
Performance measure: number of digital transformation initiatives established by client entities (cumulative)
Result 3: harmonized approach to information and communications technology security across all United Nations Office at Vienna client department locations – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29F.62	The increasing reliance on digital tools in field operations, combined with rapidly evolving threats, have significantly heightened the exposure of client departments to ICT security risks. Laptops and other computing assets used in decentralized and high-risk environments represent a key area of exposure, with implications for business continuity, data protection and operational resilience. The subprogramme has been implementing a standardized roll-out of ICT security packages to all laptops and computer assets, aligned with Secretariat-wide ICT security policies and delivered through a phased, risk-based approach.
Lessons learned and planned change

29F.63	The lesson for the subprogramme was that early engagement with field locations of client departments and the use of standardized, centrally managed security configurations were essential to ensuring consistency, scalability and sustained adoption. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will further strengthen coordination between Vienna and field locations and prioritize automation and standardization in future roll-out phases of ICT security configurations across all locations. This initiative will strengthen operational resilience, standardize security controls and support secure and reliable ICT operations in field locations to enable programme delivery.
29F.64	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29F.VII).
Figure 29F.VII
Performance measure: number of client offices at Vienna and in the field that implemented standardized configurations to enhance information and communications technology security (annual)
Deliverables
29F.65	Table 29F.14 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29F.14
Subprogramme 4: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Information and communications technology: information security for information technology systems and data; connectivity services for remote devices; hosting services; voice, video and data communications services; operation and maintenance of existing and new application systems in a secure environment; technical support for approximately 3,000 end users; and advisory services on business solutions.
